issues.comment: After `mark_committed` removes all offsets ≤ the committed value, `min_offset` shifts past any gap in the offset sequence. On the next commit-loop iteration, `start = max(last_committed + 1, min_offset)` jumps over the gap, allowing offsets beyond the gap to be committed—losing the unprocessed message forever. For example, with offsets {100, 102, 103} submitted (101 never seen), all completed: first commit correctly commits 100, then mark_committed(100) removes ≤100 leaving {102, 103}, then second get_committable_offsets computes min_offset=102, start=max(101,102)=102, and commits 103—skipping offset 101 entirely. The fix should be: when last_committed has been set for this partition, use start = last_committed + 1 unconditionally (not max with min_offset). The min_offset fallback is only correct for the initial case (last_committed == -1).

The commit loop runs on a background thread every ~1 second. After the first commit of offset 100, if the commit loop fires a second time before the assertion, it will commit 103 due to the offset gap logic bug. The test only passes if the assertion runs within the 1-second window before the next commit-loop iteration. The uptime integration test (test_thread_queue_parallel_offset_gaps) has the same issue where `assert actual_offset == 101` expects 100+1, but could be 104 if commit loop fires again. Both gap tests are flaky as a direct consequence of Bug 1 combined with background commit thread timing.

The test claims to verify 'errors in processing don't block offset commits for other messages.' However, the worker's `finally` block always calls `complete_offset`—even for the failed message. So both offsets (100 for the failed message, 101 for the successful one) are marked completed. The commit loop will commit them both. The assertion `len(committed_offsets) == 0 or test_partition not in committed_offsets` only passes because the 1-second commit-loop delay hasn't elapsed yet—it's timing-dependent, not behavior-dependent. The test is testing commit-loop timing, not error handling semantics.
